sections.paragraph: or according to הַמְנוּךְ כתיב Chald. Dan 5:7, 16 , 29 , a necklace, neckchain, monile. To this corresponds Syr. ܗܰܡܢܺܝܟܐܳ, ܗܶܡܢܺܝܟܐܳ, and Greek μανιάκης, μανίακον, μάννος: also μανάκιον, μαννάκιον: all of which are diminutives from μάνος, μάννος, μόννος, the words being chiefly Doric; whence also the Lat. monile (see Polyb. ii. 31; Pollux v. 16, the Greek interpreters of the O. T. in Biel and Schleusner). ה in Ch. and Syr. is a prosthetic letter, and ךְ or ־ִיךְ is a diminutive termination familiar to the Persians and Greeks. If the etymology of the syllable מנ μάνος be further traced, the idea of many is not improbable that it properly signifies the moon, and that μανιάκη is properly i.q. μηνίσκος, a little moon worn round the neck (compare שַׂהֲרֹון). Indeed in modern Persic the moon is called ماه, but the primitive Nun is shown to be omitted by the Greek μήν, μήνη, Dor. μάνα, Goth. mana, Lat. mensis, Germ. Mahn, Mond. Geddes on Exo 25:22, compares the Lat. manica from manus, and supposes המ׳ properly to mean an armlet. [“Comp. also Sanscr. mani, a gem, a pearl.”]
